title: GStack Pattern Synthesis for Substrate — Consolidated Final
purpose: All-encompassing baseline reference for later alternatives research on intake/planning completeness, memory, persistence, and execution structure.
what_added: Compiled template/resolver architecture as a first-class pattern.
The shared preamble as a runtime kernel for retrieval, session awareness, and operational policy.
Checkpoint/resume as a distinct artifact type, not a side effect.
Builder/developer profile memory and the ongoing migration from `builder-profile.jsonl` to `developer-profile.json`.
Global retro and cross-tool discovery as a separate meta-memory plane.
Autoplan’s explicit phase gates, required outputs, review report footer, and on-disk decision audit trail.
Telemetry separation and version-sensitive operational nuances.
LangGraph memory taxonomy and Reflexion as adjacent references alongside MemGPT and Generative Agents.
core_synthesis: gstack is best understood as a compiled artifact-first workflow runtime, not a bag of prompts.
Its strongest abstraction is the separation between methodology, runtime metadata, and persisted artifacts.

A “complete” plan in gstack is not a filled template; it is a plan that has passed the required review axes for its task class, under an explicit mode, with mandatory Step 0 outputs and no unresolved critical gaps.
Its durable memory system is layered: session awareness, project-local ledgers, checkpoints, review memory, behavioral profile memory, and global retro/meta-memory.
Compounding happens because the right slice of prior artifacts is retrieved before the next skill runs, and because the system makes that compounding visible.
The repo’s append-only, local-first persistence model is a major strength for auditability and hackability, but it also reveals where substrate should add stronger typing and validation.
The repo is currently in a mild migration state around builder-profile versus developer-profile, which is itself a useful pattern study for backward-compatible evolution.
The main ceiling in gstack is not planning taste; it is deterministic verification of machine-checkable claims.

session_types.name: /office-hours
session_types.input: user request + local repo context + prior designs/profile

session_types.name: /plan-ceo-review
session_types.axis: right problem, right scope, ambition, reversibility

session_types.name: /plan-eng-review
session_types.axis: architecture, tests, failure modes, implementation shape

session_types.name: /plan-devex-review
session_types.axis: onboarding, docs, API ergonomics, TTHW, persona fit

session_types.name: /autoplan
session_types.input: design doc + skill files + plan file
session_types.output: living reviewed plan + review logs + audit trail

session_types.name: /review
session_types.input: git diff + prior learnings + prior review memory
session_types.axis: shipped-code risk, regressions, production failure exposure

session_types.name: /checkpoint
session_types.input: current branch, working tree, conversation state

session_types.name: /retro global
session_types.input: discovered local AI-coding session metadata + repos

artifact_inventory.question: What problem are we solving, under what assumptions, with what alternatives?
artifact_inventory.notes: supports supersedes lineage; branch-scoped durable intake artifact

artifact_inventory.question: What do we know about the builder/operator across sessions?
artifact_inventory.notes: currently mid-migration from builder-profile.jsonl to developer-profile.json

artifact_inventory.question: What exactly are we building and why?
artifact_inventory.notes: living object with review report, phase outputs, and decision audit trail

artifact_inventory.question: How do non-happy paths fail and recover?
artifact_inventory.notes: focuses on user-visible rescue paths, not just exceptions

artifact_inventory.question: What realistic production failures still exist?
artifact_inventory.notes: used to surface silent-failure critical gaps

artifact_inventory.question: What must be tested and where?
Where are we right now, what was decided, and what remains?
artifact_inventory.notes: captures decisions made, remaining work, notes, modified files, duration

artifact_inventory.question: What do we know now that should compound later?
consumers: many skills via preamble or explicit search

artifact_inventory.question: What happened, in what order, on what branch?
Which gates have run, on which commit, and with what unresolved risk?
What autonomous decisions were made, under which principle?
artifact_inventory.notes: kept on disk to avoid conversation-context bloat

artifact_inventory.question: What patterns recur across repos, tools, and time windows?
How is the product being used?
artifact_inventory.notes: should be kept separate from operational memory

patterns.title: Compiled methodology / runtime / artifact separation
patterns.observed: gstack keeps the judgment-heavy workflow in `SKILL.md.tmpl`, then compiles live `SKILL.md` files using `gen-skill-docs.ts` and resolvers that inject preambles, dashboards, test bootstrap, and optional cross-model blocks.
patterns.why_it_matters: This reduces prompt drift without freezing the methodology into code. The method stays hand-authored; the operational sections stay synchronized with implementation.
patterns.carry_forward: Treat substrate planning/intake as a compiled prompt DSL or template system. Separate methodology, runtime metadata, and persisted outputs as different layers.

patterns.title: Shared preamble as runtime kernel
patterns.observed: Every skill starts with a generated preamble that performs update checks, session tracking, active-session counting, contributor mode, universal question formatting, search-before-building policy, learning lookup, and timeline writes.
patterns.why_it_matters: This makes important runtime behaviors consistent across all skills and gives memory retrieval a predictable insertion point.
patterns.carry_forward: Implement a policy-layer preamble in substrate that runs before task-specific logic: context recovery, session awareness, retrieval, telemetry gating, and standardized user decision formatting.

patterns.title: Artifact-first session typing
patterns.observed: gstack sessions are defined less by chat persona and more by what artifact they transform: office-hours creates a design doc, plan reviews enrich it, review logs persist gating results, checkpoint captures working state.
patterns.why_it_matters: This creates typed handoffs instead of loosely related conversations.
patterns.carry_forward: Define substrate session kinds as artifact transforms with required inputs, outputs, completeness gates, freshness rules, and persistence rules.

patterns.title: Intake emits multiple related artifacts, not one plan blob
patterns.observed: `/office-hours` appends a builder/developer-profile event and writes a design doc; downstream reviews discover that design automatically.
patterns.why_it_matters: Different artifacts answer different downstream questions: planning state, builder state, review state, resume state.
patterns.carry_forward: At intake time, write at least a framing artifact, a project-local design/plan artifact, and a behavioral/session event. Do not collapse them into one file.

patterns.title: Lineage is first-class
patterns.observed: Design docs can carry a `Supersedes:` link; review logs are branch+commit scoped; checkpoints capture branch and timestamp; global retros snapshot windows for later trend comparison.
patterns.why_it_matters: Lineage is what turns “more context” into trustworthy context.
patterns.carry_forward: Every substrate artifact should carry identifiers such as artifact_id, parent_ids, supersedes, session_id, project_id, branch, commit, mode, status, timestamps.

patterns.title: Search before building / landscape awareness
patterns.observed: The preamble contains a search-before-building policy; office-hours adds a dedicated landscape-awareness phase; review/search phases verify that proposed patterns are still current best practice.
patterns.why_it_matters: It prevents the workflow from locking onto stale or needlessly bespoke solutions.
patterns.carry_forward: Make “search first when unfamiliar or current-best-practice matters” a runtime rule, not an optional reviewer habit.

patterns.title: Mode selection before depth
patterns.observed: CEO review forces an explicit review posture: scope expansion, selective expansion, hold scope, or scope reduction, with defaults based on task shape and file-count heuristics.
patterns.why_it_matters: The same review posture should not serve greenfield ideation, bugfix hardening, and overbuilt-plan reduction.
patterns.carry_forward: Make planning mode explicit at intake or pre-review time. “Complete” depends on what sort of completeness the workflow is optimizing for.

patterns.title: Pre-review system audit before planning judgments
patterns.observed: CEO review begins with recent git history, diff stats, stash inspection, TODO/FIXME/HACK scans, and recently touched files before evaluating scope or architecture.
patterns.why_it_matters: This grounds the review in the actual working state and avoids plan review in a vacuum.
patterns.carry_forward: Before deep review, run a cheap system audit: branch context, diff shape, deferred backlog, pending hacks, and surrounding recent work.

patterns.title: Mandatory Step 0 before detailed review
patterns.observed: Engineering review starts with a Step 0 scope challenge: existing code reuse, minimum viable change, complexity smell test, search check, TODO cross-reference, completeness check, and distribution check.
patterns.why_it_matters: Many planning failures happen before architecture review even begins: rebuilding what exists, hiding deferrals, or ignoring distribution and silent failure.
patterns.carry_forward: Require a Step 0 contract ahead of plan detail. Substrate should not allow architecture review to start until Step 0 outputs exist.

patterns.title: Phase transitions gate on written outputs
patterns.observed: `/autoplan` refuses to begin the next phase until the current phase’s mandatory artifacts are written to the plan file and the relevant gate has passed.
patterns.why_it_matters: This is one of the strongest reasons gstack plans feel finished: handoffs are artifact-gated, not token-budget-gated.
patterns.carry_forward: In substrate, transitions should depend on persisted outputs and gate checks, not just model confidence or “I think I’m done.”

patterns.title: Completion summaries are machine-gatable handoff contracts
patterns.observed: CEO and eng reviews end with compact summaries that count issues, note whether negative-space sections were written, report gaps, diagram counts, outside-voice status, and unresolved items.
patterns.why_it_matters: A compact summary lets later stages decide what ran and what still blocks execution without re-reading the entire plan.
patterns.carry_forward: Emit structured completion summaries from every review axis. Treat them as state for downstream routing and dashboards.

patterns.title: Living plan status belongs on disk
patterns.observed: `/autoplan` can append a `GSTACK REVIEW REPORT` footer to the plan file if one is missing, making review state part of the plan’s living status.
patterns.why_it_matters: A plan is more useful when its freshness and review coverage are visible in the artifact itself.
patterns.carry_forward: Make reviewed-plan artifacts self-describing: include review state, freshness, unresolved gaps, and next-step hints inside the artifact.

patterns.title: Decision audit trail stays on disk, not in chat history
patterns.observed: `/autoplan` appends one audit-trail row per auto-decision into the plan file so decision rationale does not accumulate only in ephemeral conversation context.
patterns.why_it_matters: This preserves accountability while minimizing context-window clutter.
patterns.carry_forward: Persist autonomous decisions as a structured decision log linked to the artifact they modified.

patterns.title: Completeness is multi-axis, not scalar
patterns.observed: gstack decomposes review into strategy, engineering, design, DX, and implementation diff review rather than one universal “good plan” pass.
patterns.why_it_matters: Different failure modes require different passes. A plan can be strategically sound but implementation-incomplete or great technically but weak in DX.
patterns.carry_forward: Model completeness as the conjunction of required axes for a task class rather than a single global score.

patterns.title: Alternatives are mandatory before commitment
patterns.observed: Office-hours and CEO review require multiple materially different approaches instead of letting the first plausible path become the plan.
patterns.why_it_matters: Alternatives expose hidden assumptions and create an honest trade-space between minimal change and ideal architecture.
patterns.carry_forward: Require 2–3 materially different approaches for non-trivial work and preserve the rejected options as part of the artifact history.

patterns.title: Explicit reuse inventory
patterns.observed: Plan reviews repeatedly force a “What already exists” section mapping sub-problems to current code or infrastructure.
patterns.why_it_matters: Without an explicit reuse pass, agents routinely propose parallel systems instead of extending what is already there.
patterns.carry_forward: Every serious plan should include a reuse inventory with evidence and reuse strategy, not just a sentence saying “existing code exists.”

patterns.title: Explicit negative space / `NOT in scope`
patterns.observed: CEO and eng reviews insist on a written `NOT in scope` section and push deferred items into it rather than letting them disappear.
patterns.why_it_matters: Completeness is not “include everything”; it is “make omissions explicit and reviewable.”
patterns.carry_forward: Track deferments as first-class artifacts with rationale, not as silent absences.

patterns.title: Distribution path is part of completeness
patterns.observed: Eng review treats distribution as mandatory when a plan introduces a new artifact type such as a CLI, library, container, or mobile app: CI/CD, target platforms, and install path must be present or explicitly deferred.
patterns.why_it_matters: Code that cannot be built, published, or installed is not complete for artifact-producing work.
patterns.carry_forward: Substrate plan schemas should include distribution/publish/install fields whenever the deliverable is something users download or integrate.

patterns.title: Future-implementer temporal interrogation
patterns.observed: CEO review asks what the implementer will wish had been planned for in hour 1, hours 2–3, and hours 6+.
patterns.why_it_matters: This surfaces the details that are cheapest to specify now and most expensive to rediscover mid-execution.
patterns.carry_forward: Bake a temporal interrogation pass into plan hardening: what future execution loops will regret not having now?

patterns.title: Hidden-path rendering and mandatory diagrams
patterns.observed: CEO review requires diagrams for architecture, data flow, state machine, error flow, deployment sequence, and rollback; design/eng reviews force loading, empty, error, boundary, and partial states into view.
patterns.why_it_matters: The most dangerous implementation gaps are often the paths the plan merely implies.
patterns.carry_forward: Represent critical flows explicitly. Hidden-path rendering should be a gate, not a “nice to have.”

patterns.title: Failure-mode surfacing and silent-failure critical gaps
patterns.observed: For each new codepath, eng review asks how it can fail in production, whether a test covers it, whether error handling exists, and whether the user sees a clear error or a silent failure. No test + no handling + silent user experience becomes a critical gap.
patterns.why_it_matters: This is a practical way to convert edge cases into actionable risk state.
patterns.carry_forward: Use a failure-mode registry that links each risk to test coverage, error handling, and user-visible outcome.

patterns.title: Anti-skip rule: if nothing is wrong, say what was checked
patterns.observed: Review skills require sections with no findings to still say what was evaluated and why nothing was flagged.
patterns.why_it_matters: This prevents fake completeness where a pass is “done” without demonstrating that it actually ran.
patterns.carry_forward: Require every axis to leave a trace, even when the result is clean.

patterns.title: Planning should emit execution-lane hints
patterns.observed: Eng review includes a worktree parallelization strategy that groups work into independent lanes or explicitly marks the plan as sequential.
patterns.why_it_matters: This is the bridge from planning to a modular execution framework.
patterns.carry_forward: Have planning artifacts emit dependency lanes, critical path hints, or an execution graph rather than just a prose step list.

patterns.title: Memory is layered into planes, not one store
patterns.observed: gstack separates session awareness, project-local learnings and timelines, checkpoints, review logs, builder/developer profile data, and global retros.
patterns.why_it_matters: These stores answer different questions and should not all be queried or injected the same way.
patterns.carry_forward: Use at least session, project, builder/operator, and global scopes. Within them, separate semantic learnings, episodic timeline, checkpoints, review facts, and behavioral profile.

patterns.title: Append-only local artifacts with read-time consolidation
patterns.observed: Learnings, timeline events, review logs, builder-profile events, and checkpoints are written append-first or file-first, with dedup and summary logic happening on read.
patterns.why_it_matters: Append-only writes are robust, auditable, and concurrency-friendly enough for local agent workflows.
patterns.carry_forward: Prefer append-only event or artifact writes, then consolidate on read or in synthesis jobs. Preserve the raw trail.

patterns.title: Typed learnings with provenance, trust, and confidence
patterns.observed: Learnings carry constrained types, sources, keys, confidence, branch, commit, and related files; the logger validates and sanitizes inputs before storage.
patterns.why_it_matters: This prevents “memory” from becoming an uninspectable blob and reduces prompt-injection or low-quality carryover risk.
patterns.carry_forward: Memory records should encode what kind of thing they are, where they came from, how much to trust them, and where they apply.

patterns.title: Confidence decay and cross-project trust gating
patterns.observed: Observed and inferred learnings decay over time; cross-project retrieval is opt-in; untrusted cross-project learnings are filtered out to avoid contamination.
patterns.why_it_matters: Portable memory should be harder to earn than local memory.
patterns.carry_forward: Make portability, trust tier, and decay policy explicit fields, not retrieval-side guesswork.

patterns.title: Context recovery and cross-session injection belong in the preamble
patterns.observed: Session intelligence proposes and skills implement a preamble that reads recent plans, reviews, checkpoints, and timelines after compaction or on session start, and can emit a `Last session:` one-liner.
patterns.why_it_matters: Memory only compounds if the right slice is loaded before the next reasoning step.
patterns.carry_forward: Put retrieval policy in a pre-task context composer rather than relying on each planner prompt to remember to retrieve the right things.

patterns.title: Visible compounding beats silent compounding
patterns.observed: When a current review finding matches a past learning, gstack tells the user: “Prior learning applied …” so compounding is visible.
patterns.why_it_matters: Users need to see that memory helped, otherwise memory becomes an invisible source of unexplained behavior.
patterns.carry_forward: Expose retrieved-memory influence explicitly when it changes the current review or plan.

patterns.title: Checkpoint/resume is a first-class artifact
patterns.observed: `/checkpoint` writes a durable markdown artifact with summary, decisions made, remaining work, notes, files modified, branch, timestamp, and session duration; resume mode reads it back and warns on branch mismatch.
patterns.why_it_matters: This is what makes long-running work and cross-branch handoffs survivable.
patterns.carry_forward: Keep checkpoint artifacts distinct from plans and learnings. They answer “where was I?” not “what do we believe?”

patterns.title: Behavioral builder/developer profile is its own memory plane
patterns.observed: Office-hours appends builder-profile events; the repo now includes a unified developer-profile with tiers, accumulated signals, cross-project detection, resources shown, inferred dimensions, and mismatch checks.
patterns.why_it_matters: Some useful memory is about how the operator works, not about the codebase.
patterns.carry_forward: Keep operator-profile memory separate from project memory. Use it for guidance, tuning, and resource curation, not for code facts.

patterns.title: Global retros provide cross-project meta-memory
patterns.observed: `/retro global` plus `gstack-global-discover` scan local tool metadata from multiple AI coding tools, resolve repos, deduplicate by normalized remote URL, and save cross-project snapshots with session and streak metrics.
patterns.why_it_matters: This creates a higher plane of memory about how the builder works across repos and tools, not just within one project.
patterns.carry_forward: If substrate needs cross-project learning, distinguish project memory from behavioral meta-memory and keep their retrieval/use cases separate.

patterns.title: Telemetry is separate from operational memory
patterns.observed: Config exposes telemetry modes (`off | anonymous | community`), and skill preambles gate analytics/remote telemetry behavior separately from project-local learnings and timelines.
patterns.why_it_matters: Product analytics and durable task memory should not be the same system.
patterns.carry_forward: Keep analytics, memory, and review state as separate stores with separate policies and consent models.

patterns.title: Review memory is release-gating memory
patterns.observed: Review log scripts store branch-scoped JSONL facts that later phases and `/ship` can use to know which gates ran, on what commit, and with what unresolved risk.
patterns.why_it_matters: Knowing that “a review happened once” is not enough; the system needs commit-aware review freshness.
patterns.carry_forward: Persist review facts as their own memory class with branch, commit, status, unresolved counts, and source phase.

patterns.title: Independent voices are useful when they remain independent
patterns.observed: `/autoplan` runs Codex and a Claude subagent independently, treats outside-voice findings as informational until explicitly accepted, and logs consensus/disagreement instead of silently merging everything.
patterns.why_it_matters: Agreement is signal; disagreement reveals judgment boundaries.
patterns.carry_forward: Treat multi-voice review as a consensus engine with explicit merge rules and disagreement surfacing, not as invisible ensemble averaging.

patterns.title: Parallelize by dependency, not enthusiasm
patterns.observed: Specialist subagents can run in parallel, but major `autoplan` phases are explicitly serialized because each phase depends on written outputs from the previous one.
patterns.why_it_matters: Blind parallelism often shifts cost into reconciliation.
patterns.carry_forward: Only parallelize independent lanes. Serialize artifact-dependent stages and use the plan’s execution graph to decide where concurrency is actually safe.

patterns.title: Tier ceremony to task complexity
patterns.observed: OpenClaw/gstack-lite patterns show a reduced-ceremony path for simple work and fuller pipelines for larger or more ambiguous work.
patterns.why_it_matters: The full process is too heavy for small changes, but too little structure creates ambiguity debt on large work.
patterns.carry_forward: Add explicit ceremony tiers based on change size, uncertainty, and artifact requirements.

patterns.title: Prompt-only structure has a ceiling
patterns.observed: Issue reports show that even a strong review structure can miss wrong file paths, math, CLI flags, and other machine-verifiable claims when the system relies only on narrative review.
patterns.why_it_matters: A plan can sound complete while still being concretely wrong.
patterns.carry_forward: Separate narrative review from deterministic verification. Substrate should lint or verify concrete claims such as file paths, APIs, CLI flags, package versions, and arithmetic.

methods.name: Six forcing questions with smart routing
methods.summary: Ask one forcing question at a time; route only the relevant subset based on stage (pre-product, has users, paying customers, pure infra).

methods.name: Related-design discovery before starting fresh
methods.summary: After the user states the problem, search prior design docs for keyword overlap and ask whether to build on them or start fresh.

methods.name: Landscape awareness / search before building
methods.summary: After understanding the problem, search conventional wisdom and recent best practice before committing to infrastructure or architecture choices.

methods.name: Mode selection card
methods.summary: Force an explicit review posture: scope expansion, selective expansion, hold scope, or scope reduction.

methods.name: Step 0 scope challenge
methods.summary: Before detailed review, answer: what already exists, minimum viable change, complexity smell, search check, deferred backlog, completeness check, distribution path.

methods.name: Alternatives-first gate
methods.summary: Require 2–3 materially different approaches with effort, risk, pros/cons, and reuse story before locking the plan.

methods.name: Reuse inventory
methods.summary: Map each sub-problem to existing code, assets, or flows that can be extended rather than rebuilt.

methods.name: Negative-space section
methods.summary: Write a `NOT in scope` section with rationale for every deferred item.

methods.name: Distribution gate
methods.summary: If the deliverable is a CLI, library, package, container, mobile app, or similar artifact, require build/publish/install details or explicitly defer them.

methods.name: Temporal interrogation
methods.summary: Ask what the implementer will wish had been planned for in hour 1, hours 2–3, and hours 6+.

methods.name: Happy / nil / empty / error / partial rendering
methods.summary: For every new flow or user-facing state, render the path instead of implying it.

methods.name: Failure-mode registry
methods.summary: For each new codepath, list a realistic production failure and whether it has test coverage, error handling, and clear user-visible behavior.

methods.name: Phase-transition summary
methods.summary: End each phase with a written summary and do not advance until the required outputs are on disk.

methods.name: Decision audit trail row
methods.summary: For every autonomous decision, write phase, classification, principle, rationale, and rejected alternative to an audit table on disk.

methods.name: Prior-learning callout
methods.summary: When a current finding matches prior learning, say so explicitly with key, confidence, and date.

methods.name: Checkpoint template
methods.summary: Persist summary, decisions made, remaining work, notes, modified files, branch, timestamp, and duration as a resumable artifact.

methods.name: After-action reflection into operational learnings
methods.summary: At skill end, reflect on failures, wrong approaches, and project quirks, then log them as operational learnings.

methods.name: DX persona and TTHW target
methods.summary: Choose the developer persona and target time-to-hello-world tier before reviewing docs, onboarding, and API ergonomics.

methods.name: Cross-phase theme extraction
methods.summary: Surface concerns that appeared independently in two or more phases as high-confidence themes.

gate_checks: all required review axes ran for the task class
all mandatory outputs exist on disk
staleness / commit drift is below threshold or review re-ran
decision log and completion summary were written

evaluation_questions: Does the alternative separate methodology, runtime metadata, and persisted artifacts, or does it collapse everything into prompts?
Does intake emit multiple related artifacts (design/plan, behavioral/session event, lineage metadata), or just one document?
Can the alternative enforce mode selection and a mandatory Step 0 before detailed review?
Does it require negative-space outputs such as reuse inventory, `NOT in scope`, distribution path, and failure modes?
Can it express completeness as required review axes rather than a single generic score?
Does it support append-only lineage and read-time or synthesis-time consolidation?
Does memory have distinct scopes or planes (session, project, builder/operator, global) with explicit trust and portability?
Can the system recover context after compaction or session loss via checkpoints, timeline, and recent artifact injection?
Does it persist review/gate state with commit awareness so later phases can reason about freshness?
Can it expose which memories were applied so compounding is visible to operators?
Does it distinguish telemetry/product analytics from operational memory and planning artifacts?
Does it pair narrative planning/review with deterministic verification for machine-checkable claims?

risks.title: Hidden storage hurts discoverability
risks.summary: Persisting value mostly under `~/.gstack/` is practical, but users can lose track of where the canonical artifacts live.

risks.title: Canonical-source drift is a real risk
risks.summary: When multiple plan or design file locations can exist (`.gstack`, `.claude/plans`, etc.), downstream skills may read the wrong one unless there is a single canonical artifact ID/path.

risks.title: Workflow-state resume needs stronger machine state
risks.summary: Checkpoint and review memory help, but issue reports show that users still want more explicit workflow-state tracking for session resume.

risks.title: Behavioral profile is mid-migration
risks.summary: The repo currently bridges legacy `builder-profile.jsonl` and a newer `developer-profile.json`; office-hours still appends builder-profile events while reader tooling is already migrating to the unified profile.

risks.title: Local-first JSONL is excellent for auditability but lightly structured
risks.summary: The file-based append-only model is easy to inspect, grep, and debug, but it will need stronger typing, migration handling, and concurrency semantics if substrate becomes a shared multi-crate core.

risks.title: Prompt compliance is not the same as validation
risks.summary: Even strong prompts can omit required fields or approve concrete claims that should have been machine-checked.

risks.title: Telemetry and analytics behavior is version-sensitive
risks.summary: Config and current preambles clearly separate telemetry settings from operational memory, but analytics behaviors should still be treated as version-sensitive and policy-controlled.

adjacent_systems.title: Every compound-knowledge-plugin
adjacent_systems.fit: Reinforces the value of git-tracked knowledge artifacts, multi-granularity plans, and explicit compounding across work sessions.

adjacent_systems.title: Compound Engineering (Every)
adjacent_systems.fit: Sharpens outcome-focused instructions and parallel workstream thinking that pair well with execution-lane planning.

adjacent_systems.title: LangGraph memory taxonomy
adjacent_systems.fit: Adds a clean split between thread-scoped short-term memory and namespace-scoped long-term memory, plus a useful memory-class vocabulary.

adjacent_systems.title: MemGPT
adjacent_systems.fit: Contributes the hot/warm/cold or tiered-memory metaphor for tight context injection.

adjacent_systems.title: Generative Agents
adjacent_systems.fit: Adds the reflection layer: raw event streams become more useful when periodically synthesized into higher-order learnings that guide planning.

adjacent_systems.title: Reflexion
adjacent_systems.fit: Strengthens the case for an explicit after-action reflection artifact or service for repeated task families.
